summary:
The domestication of sheep from the wild mouflon (Ovis orientalis) represents one of the foundational achievements of the Neolithic Revolution in southwestern Asia. Archaeological and genetic evidence places the primary domestication event in the Zagros Mountains and surrounding areas of northern Mesopotamia, with the process well underway by approximately 9000–8000 BCE. Early domesticated sheep are identified in the archaeological record through changes in herd demographics — a higher proportion of young males culled for meat — and eventual morphological changes in skeletal structure and horn form.

Sheep provided early farming communities with a uniquely versatile resource base. In addition to meat, they offered milk, blood, hide, and — as wool-producing breeds were later selected — fiber for textiles. This multi-product utility made sheep central to the mixed agropastoral economy that characterized Neolithic communities across the Fertile Crescent and, subsequently, much of the Old World.

The domestication of sheep occurred roughly concurrently with that of [[event:1258|goats in the Zagros Mountains]], and both species frequently appear together in the faunal assemblages of early Neolithic sites such as [[event:1358|Chogha Golan]] and Ali Kosh. Together, the management of small ruminants transformed human subsistence strategies and laid the foundation for pastoralism — a mobile herding lifestyle that would become a major adaptive strategy across arid and semi-arid zones of Eurasia and Africa.
